steps.prompt: Which words form the main verb?

hints.est: 'Est' alone is the auxiliary — together with 'Factus' it forms the perfect passive
hints.sudor: 'Sudor' is a noun meaning 'sweat' — the subject

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'Factus est' translates to?

hints.to make / to do: 'To make' is 'facere' (active) — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri' (passive/become)
hints.to carry: 'To carry' is 'ferre' — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri'
hints.to trust: 'To trust' is 'fidere' — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri'

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'Factus est'?
steps.reveal: Factus est = became / was made (3rd sing. perf.)

hints.present indicative: Present of 'fio' would be 'fit' — 'factus est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'factus erat' — 'factus est' is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'factus sit' — 'factus est' is indicative

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the active parts of 'facio' — 'fio' serves as its passive
hints.fallo, fallere, fefelli, falsum: These are the parts of 'fallo' (to deceive)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'fero' (to carry)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'sudoris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sudoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'sudoris' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'is/ea/id' — 'eius' is the genitive singular

hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular 'guttae' looks the same, but here it is the comparandum after 'sicut' — nominative plural
hints.dative singular: Dative singular 'guttae' looks the same, but the sense requires nominative plural (drops)
hints.vocative plural: Vocative plural 'guttae' looks the same, but this is not direct address

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'guttae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'guttae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'guttae' ends in -ae

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'sanguinis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sanguinis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'sanguinis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What literary device does 'sicut guttae sanguinis' employ?
steps.reveal: His sweat became like drops of blood

hints.metaphor: A metaphor identifies one thing as another without 'like/as' — 'sicut' signals a simile
hints.metonymy: Metonymy substitutes a related word — 'sicut' introduces a direct comparison
hints.synecdoche: Synecdoche uses a part for the whole — this is a comparison using 'sicut'

translation: His sweat became like drops of blood
